oai_dc:dc.dc:title: AFA Disaster Relief Coordinates Efforts For the Maui Fire Victims
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Pendergrass, Nick
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: This article originally appeared in AFA Watchbird Volume 51 Number 3 (July 2024). The text below contains excerpts from the original article. For photographs, figures, advertisements, and the complete formatted version as originally published, please use the PDF link associated with this article. On the morning of August 8, 2023, David Vanzo and Barrie Matthews, the owners and operators of The Lahaina Bird Stand, woke up to 80 mph winds whipping through their Maui neighborhood. With gusts up to 100 mph, trees were being ripped up all over and David began trimming one of their trees to prevent it from smashing into the house. At that point, he could see the intense black smoke filling the air over his roof. "It was a north fire, moving west. We were south, and it just wasn't coming our way," said Matthews in an interview after the fire. "The fire, I believe at one point they said it was moving like a mile a minute. It was covering ground extremely fast," continued Vanzo. "We set a marker on a tree and we said, if that smoke comes anywhere near that tree we're out of here." With little time to spare, David and Barrie loaded up their 15 birds, 4 dogs, and as many cats as they could fit and fled down an emergency exit road not knowing if they would have anything to return to. After the fires, a local jewelry store owner offered them a place to stay but the reality of adding 3 people and all those animals to the household was too much. But then, Barrie got a call from their vet who took the 4 dogs, 3 cats and all 15 of their birds into their facility - changing the cat room into a bird room and modifying cages to accept perches. The birds were then moved to temporary housing at the Hyatt Regency Maui Resort and Spa who closed to tourists to help those displaced by the fires. AFA Disaster Relief reached out via social media to anyone they could reach in the state to try to assist David, Barrie and anyone else in need. AFA made contact with Lea Hollingsworth-Ramsey with the Hawaiian Feathered Friends Network to assess not only what was needed, but the logistics of transporting goods from the mainland to the ravaged Island. AFA Disaster Relief, The Hawaiian Feathered Friends Network, The Parrot Posse, My Safe Bird Store, and A&E Cage Co. combined forces to get David and Barrie 15 cages, with Hollingsworth-Ramsey and the Feathered Friends Network helping to coordinate the delivery and assembly for the displaced birds. Click the PDF link associated with this article to view the complete article as originally published in AFA Watchbird.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: AFA Watchbird; Vol. 51 No. 3 (2024): AFA Watchbird July 2024
